title: Functional Characterization of 7 Local Greek Tomato Cultivars in Ripening
text:
This page will be updated with further details upon the completion of the project.
This ongoing project focuses on the functional characterization of Greek tomato landraces during fruit ripening. Tomato landraces were chosen an they represent valuable genetic resources as they carry useful variation in ripening speed, firmness retention, fruit quality, and shelf-life-related traits.
I am comparing the selected Greek tomato landraces Aspros Lotos, Pantaroza Pink, Lemonati, Kardia Vodiou, Pantaroza Red, Yellow Santorini, and Karabola, together with commercial / well characterized cultivars used as controls, including Ailsa Craig and Makedonia.
At the current stage, the work is focused on monitoring fruits across ripening stages, and the main parameters recorded are fruit weight, firmness progression using Durofel, and ethylene production from bbreaker to red ripe. These measurements will be linked with their related gene expression analysis and used to identify differences among genotypes in ripening behavior and postharvest performance.
The next step will include gene expression analysis related to lycopene content and volatile profiling and it will contribute to a better understanding of ripening diversity in Greek tomato germplasm.

title: Learning R & Data Science
text:
Although working with R feels much more natural to me now, learning it was not always easy. The breakthrough for me what when I signed up for Kane’s Data Science Course. What made the bootcamp special was its collaborative environment. The classes brought together people with different levels of experience, which made learning feel more accessible and encouraging. Seeing how others approached the same problems helped me understand how to think more clearly about data analysis. As a final project for the bootcamp, I created my first R-based project: Phyllosphere Microbiome. Looking back at it now, I can clearly see things I would improve, both in the analysis and in the presentation. Still, it remains an important milestone for me.
Some resources that I have found incredibly useful while learning include From Data to Viz, a practical guide that helps users choose suitable graph types based on their data structure while also pointing out common visualization pitfalls, and ggplot2 Uncharted, an online course focused on strengthening data visualization skills in R through ggplot2.
In the following months, I plan to continue improving my understanding of statistics, moving from frequentist approaches toward Bayesian thinking. I will be sharing small projects made along the way as part of this learning process.

title: My Path in Plant Science
text:
My academic journey began at the Bizerte Preparatory Engineering Institute, where my interest in plant biology first started to take shape through the foundational courses I had taken there. This growing curiosity became the main reason I chose to pursue agronomy after the national examination, within the Tunisian Grandes Écoles system, which guides students toward specialized engineering schools based on their ranking and academic orientation. I then joined the Higher Institute of Agronomic Sciences of Chott Mariem, where I specialized in Horticultural Production and developed a stronger connection between plant science, crop management, and applied agronomy. A key step in this journey was my selection for the Erasmus+ exchange program at Agrocampus Ouest Rennes-Angers in France. This experience introduced me to biotechnology-oriented laboratory training and encouraged me to think about plant science from a broader perspective. It also strengthened my interest in multicultural academic environments, where scientific learning is enriched by cultural exchange and collaboration.
My graduation project on Rosa centifolia was motivated by my interest in high-value horticultural species and the role of plant biotechnology in improving propagation and production practices. Working with tissue culture allowed me to explore how laboratory-based techniques can address practical challenges in ornamental and aromatic plant production. Later, my MSc research at the Mediterranean Agronomic Institute of Chania offered a continuation of this path, linking molecular plant biology with agronomic applications through the study of tomato ripening, fruit quality, and postharvest behaviour.
The following interactive timeline and map highlight the places, institutions, and experiences that have shaped my development as a plant scientist.

title: Welcome to my personal website!
text:
Welcome to my personal website!
I am Yesmine Ayeb, an Agronomy Engineer currently pursuing an MSc in Horticultural Genetics & Biotechnology at CIHEAM MAICh in Chania, Greece.
My background combines horticultural production and plant molecular biology. Through my studies and research experiences, I have developed a strong interest in plant responses to stress and the phytomicrobibome, although I am often intrigued by a variety of other plant-related topics too.
This website is still under construction and getting updated in the rare free moments I have (aka waiting for incubation to finish). If you have any feedback, please don’t hesitate to reach out.

title: Optimization of Tissue Culture Protocol for Rosa centifolia
text:
Roses are often considered recalcitrant in tissue culture because their in vitro response is highly genotype-dependent and frequently limited by contamination, oxidative browning, poor explant recovery, and inconsistent shoot regeneration. This makes the initiation phase a major bottleneck in the development of reliable micropropagation protocols. Improving rose tissue culture is therefore important because conventional vegetative propagation can be slow, season-dependent, and unable to guarantee disease-free material, whereas in vitro propagation offers the possibility of rapid clonal multiplication, pathogen-free plant production, conservation of valuable cultivars, and large-scale propagation of ornamental and aromatic roses.
In this context, my project focused on improving the initiation phase of micropropagation in Rosa centifolia, a fragrant rose species valued for ornamental, cosmetic, and pharmaceutical uses. The work aimed to optimize auxin concentration during culture initiation and to assess whether alternative antimicrobial treatments, including rosemary essential oil, thyme essential oil, and blue-green microalgae, could help reduce contamination and support the establishment of healthy explants under controlled in vitro conditions.
To address these objectives, the work was organized into three experimental assays using shoot-tip explants carrying axillary buds. In the first assay, explants were surface-sterilized and cultivated on MS media supplemented with different IBA concentrations: 0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, and 1 mg/L. The main parameters evaluated after culture initiation were infection rate, vegetative recovery, oxidative browning, number of axillary shoots, and shoot height. The results showed that IBA concentration had a clear influence on oxidative browning. Browning was significantly reduced at higher IBA concentrations and was completely eliminated from 0.75 mg/L IBA onward. Among the tested concentrations, 0.75 mg/L IBA appeared to be the best compromise because it limited browning while maintaining vegetative recovery and supporting shoot development. In contrast, 1 mg/L IBA also prevented browning but was associated with a lack of vegetative recovery, suggesting an inhibitory effect at this concentration.
The second part of the work assessed whether rosemary essential oil, thyme essential oil, and blue-green microalgae could act as alternative antimicrobial treatments. These treatments were tested at 100 ppm and 1000 ppm on MS medium supplemented with 0.75 mg/L IBA, which had been selected based on the first assay. The results showed that the alternative treatments were not sufficient to fully replace the standard laboratory disinfection protocol. In assays where explants were not properly disinfected, contamination remained very high or complete, especially in the non-disinfected control. Although some treatments showed intermediate infection levels, none provided reliable control of contamination comparable to standard sterilization.
Overall, the study confirmed that the initiation phase of Rosa centifolia micropropagation is strongly limited by the balance between contamination control, oxidative browning, and explant survival. The most promising result was obtained with MS medium supplemented with 0.75 mg/L IBA, which reduced browning without completely suppressing vegetative recovery. However, the tested essential oils and microalgae could not be recommended as standalone substitutes for conventional antimicrobial treatments. Future optimization should therefore focus on combining milder disinfection procedures with anti-browning strategies and growth regulator adjustments to improve both explant survival and regeneration efficiency.
